mappings.rationaleAI: Covers the full scope of Section 531: basic economic concepts including production, distribution, and scarcity (A); levels of economic analysis from micro through macro (B); and comparison of market, mixed, and planned economic systems (C).

Directly covers A (basic concepts through history of economic thought), B (micro and macroeconomics as analytical levels), and C (comparison of systems from classical to Keynesian and post-2008 frameworks).

Microeconomics directly covers B.1 (microeconomic analysis of individual decisions) and foundational concepts in A including scarcity/choice (A.1), diminishing returns/optimization (A.3), marginality (A.4), and competition (A.6) through supply-demand and market structure models.

Directly covers B.3 macroeconomics as the economy-as-a-whole level of analysis, including Keynesian, monetarist, and new classical schools; also addresses A.8 concepts of growth and development through GDP and long-run growth analysis.

The Wealth of Nations covers A.2 division of labour as Smith's central mechanism, A.1 resource allocation through markets, A.7 comparative advantage through free trade arguments, and underpins C.2 Western-type market economies as the foundational text of liberal economic thought.

Covers C.1.b the centrally planned economy (pure socialist model) and its variants including market-socialist models (C.1.c), and C.3 Soviet- and socialist-type economic systems through democratic socialism, Leninism, and syndicalism.

Supply and demand is the foundational model of A.1 resource allocation under scarcity and A.6 competition in markets; it underlies C.1.a the pure private enterprise economy as the price-clearing mechanism of competitive markets.
Covers A.2 division of labour as a core analytical concept across the social sciences, tracing from subsistence labor through industrial specialization to contemporary automation debates.

Business covers the structures and operations of private profit-seeking enterprises, directly illustrating C.1.a the pure private enterprise economy and the institutional form of C.2 Western-type market economies.

Capitalism directly covers C.1.a the pure private enterprise economy model and C.2 Western-type market economies, detailing private ownership, wage labor, profit motive, and market competition as organizing principles.

Communism covers C.1.b the centrally planned economy as the pure socialist model and C.3 Soviet- and socialist-type economic systems through Marxism, Leninism, Stalinism, and Maoism as implemented planned economies.

Property rights are central to A.5 the concept of capital and distinguish C.1.a the pure private enterprise economy (private property) from C.1.b the centrally planned economy (social/state ownership), with Locke, Marx, and Proudhon providing the theoretical range.

Trade covers A.7 comparative advantage as the basis for specialization and exchange, and A.8 growth and development through the relationship between trade openness and economic expansion across historical periods.

Marx covers C.1.b the pure socialist model through historical materialism and the transition to communism, C.3 Soviet-type systems as founded on Marxist theory, and A.8 growth and development through critique of capitalist accumulation and the theory of surplus value.

Stalin's five-year plans and forced collectivization are the primary historical implementation of C.1.b the centrally planned economy and C.3 Soviet-type economic systems, making this article essential for the applied dimension of these outline paths.

Finance covers A.5 the concept of capital through investment, asset valuation, and capital allocation; it also informs B.1 microeconomic decisions of firms regarding financing and resource allocation.

Poverty is central to A.8 the concepts of growth and development as both a target and a measure, and C.4 mixed economies in developing countries where poverty reduction strategies shape economic policy design.

Welfare spending is a defining feature of C.1.c the mixed economy with various degrees of economic planning, representing the state's redistribution role in capitalist economies with social provision.

Society's typology from hunting-gathering through agricultural to industrial economies covers A (basic economic concepts embedded in social structure) and C.5.a primitive economic systems as early societal formations.

Ancient history covers C.5.a primitive economic systems through the Neolithic Revolution, early trade networks, Bronze Age economies, and pre-market resource exchange across Mesopotamia, Egypt, and beyond.

The Great Depression is the central empirical case for B.3 macroeconomics (Keynesian vs. monetarist causal debates, unemployment, deflation) and shaped the development of C.2 Western-type market economies through the New Deal and subsequent regulatory frameworks.

Globalization covers A.7 comparative advantage as the driver of international specialization, A.8 growth and development through trade-led expansion, and B.2 sectoral economics through the reorganization of industries across global value chains.

Decolonization directly shaped C.4 mixed economies in developing countries, as newly independent states inherited colonial economic structures and pursued various mixed and planned strategies for development.

Singapore's Four Asian Tigers transformation exemplifies C.2 Western-type market economies in Asia and A.8 the concept of growth and development through export-led industrialization and state-guided market mechanisms.
Indonesia as Southeast Asia's largest economy with a resource-rich mixed economy under successive political regimes directly exemplifies C.4 mixed economies in developing countries.

Malaysia's upper-middle-income mixed economy with Bumiputera affirmative economic policies and export-oriented manufacturing exemplifies C.4 mixed economies in developing countries balancing market forces with planning.

East Asia encompasses the world's largest market economies (China, Japan, South Korea) and illustrates C.2 Western-type market economies alongside state-led variants, and A.8 growth and development through the 20th-century economic dynamism of the region.
